# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Secondhand Lions (2003)

**Score:** 57
**Body:** **Secondhand Lions is a feel good movie**

It does not get talked about nearly enough, but it has that rare mix of warmth, adventure, humor, family drama, and old-school storytelling.

I will not spoil the plot, but it is basically about a kid, two eccentric old men, and a summer that turns into something much bigger than expected.

It is not trying to be edgy or overly clever. It is just a sincere, charming movie with heart.

Give it a shot if you have not seen it. It is one of those films that leaves you smiling.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Totally agree. I love that movie.
2. i had never heard of it until my neighbor lent me the DVD - he actually worked on the movie (grip)

it's an fantastic movie!
3. We have had the dvd for years and watched it at least 6 times. Wonderful movie for kids and grandkids, too. 
4. Such a good movie; hits so many chords for me 
5. I read Four Lions rather than Secondhand Lions in the title. 


"that rare mix of warmth, adventure, humor, family drama, and old-school storytelling" really threw me. 
6. Great movie! It was one of my grandpa's favorite movies. I have wonderful memories of watching it with him as a child.
7. I recommend this movie alot.
8. Great Movie! Robert Duvall and Michael Caine are incredible in this movie.
9. This is one of those movies that is both appropriate and appealing for all ages. That's a hard thing to achieve and I really appreciate it. 
10. When I read.... "a summer that turns into something much bigger than expected" ... i instantly thought of Stephen King's,  Hearts In Atlantis. That is a wonderful movie

If it's anything like HIA i must watch, even if its not supernatural. Ive been meaning to see SHL for 15 years or so, im aware of it. Looks like i need to moce it up the priority list?!

## 2. Movies about animals or animals and their relations with humans?

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Hello, so I’m currently on the lookout for some films that focus on animals or at least have them as a subplot. While I love documentaries, I’m currently interested in fiction. I would prefer movies aimed towards an older audience but I know a lot of animal focused films are going to be geared to children, so that’s fine. Any type of animal is fine, wether fantasy or real, and any portrayal besides just making the animal a savage beast or a simple cute pet is fine too, although I do have a soft spot for more realistic behaviours. Dragon, horse, and dog movies are fine, but I know of a lot of the more common ones so I would prefer recommendations of lesser known ones. I really loved the first Fantastic Beasts movie, and the new Soul of An Octopus was great, really just trying to expand my palate because despite my love for animal and all the documentaries/books/games that I’ve consumed about them, the list of animal focused movies I’ve watched is quite short. 

\*and no movies where the characters are super anthropomorphized like Zootopia pls! 





### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I just watched *Remarkably Bright Creatures* which features an octopus. Fantastic film.
2. Babe (1998) - about a pig
3. Hear me out- The Last Unicorn
4. Flow
5. Good Boy 
6. Old Yeller (1957)

Homeward Bound the incredible journey (1993)
7. Fly Away Home

Congo 

Free Willy

8. Hidalgo, starring Viggo Mortensen and his mustang.
9. Pokémon Detective Pikachu / Song of the Sea / My Neighbor Totoro
10. Okja (2017)

First Cow (2019)

Whale Rider (2002)

The Bear (1988)

## 3. Who is Killing the Great Chefs of Europe? ⭐️

**Score:** 19
**Body:** *Who is Killing the Great Chefs of Europe? 1978*

This is a fun to watch black comedy. It’s got 77% on the Rotten Tomatoes meter and I always found it really charming and kind of nostalgic. It is a good one to watch with family and has a super European vibe, since it’s set there. It’s got nice film grain and cinematography, and is a classy caper in the style of murder mystery. But, most importantly, it’s funny. 😉

https://www.rottentomatoes.com/m/who\_is\_killing\_the\_great\_chefs\_of\_europe

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. definitely!
2. I remember watching this as a kid with the whole family, and all of us enjoying it.
3. The pressed duck.  
  - The Bombe
4. The book it is based on is a pretty good read!
5. Oh you just brought back a wonderful childhood memory …I LOVED that move…I had completely forgotten about it until now!
6. You brought back the memory of seeing this at the cinema. I would have been 10yo. The only image I can recall was the look on Robert Morley's face when (I think) a sauce was being squeezed. 

Who would it be these days? Ramsay, Pierre White, Blumenthal, the Fallow boys?? Sadly, I only know the celeb chefs... 

## 4. Movies like Project Hail Mary?

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I just finished it and I'm utterly overwhelmed. Amazing movie, not a second of it was not meaningful, and the cinematography is breathtaking.

The movies I can name from the top of my head that is similar to it is Interstellar and The Martian, those are the kind of movies I'm looking for—I guess I'm just looking for something that's sci-fi and involves astronauts, because those two movies are completely unrelated when it comes to the plot.

So, please, feel free to recommend anything of the sort!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Moon
2. Not space film per se. But “Arrival” is a great sci-fi film.  And gives you that intrigue and discovery feeling !
3. 2001: A Space Odyssey

The Andromeda Strain

Silent Running

Solaris

The Right Stuff

Royal Space Force: The Wings Of Honneamise

Aim For The Top! GunBuster

Contact

Gravity
  - The Andromeda Strain is not astronauts at all but it is a great recommendation
4. Sunshine
5. If you like a space horror; Event Horizon, Life, Sunshine.

Space reality: Apollo 13, First Man.

Space Oddity; Moon. Ad Astra.

Oh, Gravity is a peach, too.
6. Space Cowboys, The Astronaut Farmer, October Sky, Apollo 13...
7. Don't sleep on the real thing. The Apollo 11 doc from 2019 is great!
8. Arrival 
9. There’s a Chinese movie called *Moon Man* that’s part sci-fi part comedy.

## 5. Help! Need boring recommendations for movie

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I’m at the beach with my sister and brother in law and we can’t agree on anything to watch. My BIL doesn’t like anything gory—like ANY blood or horror. My sister is of the lifetime/hallmark variety and I like edgy stuff but at this point I don’t even care. 

Is there a movie or series that they might like? Absolutely nothing too “weird”. They are from Wisconsin if that helps. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pay for the pay per view and watch Project Hail Mary. Everyone should like that
2. The answer is always shrek. Or clue.
  - Shrek might be too spicy for the cheese heads 😂
3. People from Wisconsin can be weird and edgy. Just sayin’

Maybe Song Sung Blue. It takes place IN Wisconsin. Oscar-nominated performance by Kate Hudson.
4. Chef
5. Nobody’s Fool - small town story with Paul Newman. Absolute banger of a movie.
6. Jerry and Marge Go Large (2022) is wholesome midwestern humor. 

The Secret Life of Walter Mitty (2013) 
7. Hunt for the Wilderpeople was funny and cutesy enough that your sister would like it but also well written & directed you shouldn’t totally be bored. My husband and I (45 & 35) watch it with my parents (in their late 50’s) and his parents (early 70’s) and everyone enjoyed it.
8. Music and Lyrics
9. Flow.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What director, in your opinion, has fallen off lately despite having a strong filmography in the past? In my opinion, it’s Taika Waititi.

**Score:** 1996
**Body:** Thor: Ragnarok, What We Do in the Shadows, and Jojo Rabbit were all fun movies with solid storytelling. He had a very unique charm and style, but recently it feels like he’s lost his touch. Love and Thunder was such a mess, and the movie felt devoid of the fun and heart that his earlier films had.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Robert Zemeckis is the guy I always think of. Dude has fallen off a CLIFF in the last 20 years. 
  - Agreed. He’s directed my favorite movie, Back to the Future, and my least favorite, Pinocchio. 
2. Maybe it’s just me but I haven’t cared about anything Tim Burton has done in a while. I did not like beetlejuice beetlejuice. I want to say Big Fish was the last film I really liked of his, but I did enjoy the first season of Wednesday. I’ve probably forgotten something tbh but that’s who came to mind. 
3. ![gif](giphy|qcJxHU3DpEQCYm8YpT)

I feel this was his reaction to the reception for Ragnarok and then he decided to go all in for Love and Thunder
4. Wanna hear something kinda crazy? His quality drop off coincides with his marriage breaking up. 

Even more interesting? His wife was also his *producer*. She produced WWDITS, which is really what catapulted Taika into mainstream success. 

Jojo Rabbit was his last good film which she *also* produced and came out a year after they broke up but she was still involved with it professionally. 

  - >His quality drop off coincides with his marriage breaking up.

See also Mike Myers and George Lucas
    - Did Mike Myers break up with George Lucas?
      - ![gif](giphy|NFxJmZwnsnVeg)
  - I used to work in the film industry in NZ and was told this a long time ago (ie pre breakup). She was responsible for reigning him in A LOT and didn’t get nearly enough credit for the partnership 
5. Kubrick, dude hasnt done anything in 20 years

## 4. Remembering Bill Paxton on what could’ve been his 71st birthday. He was born on May 17th, 1955. He died on February 25th, 2017. What’s your favorite role from his filmography?

**Score:** 516
**Body:** I’ll start. Bill Harding in Twister. I also liked him in The Terminator as the Punk leader and Fred Haise in Apollo 13. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Game over man! game over!!
what the f@c& are we gonna do now?!?
  - I loved his character in that movie.  He first comes off as cocky and brash, then he starts to break when shit hits the fan.  

One of my favorite parts in the movie is when he starts bugging out when he realizes rescue won't come for 17 days.  

Ripley, "This little girl survived longer than that with no weapons and no training"

Hudson, "Why don't you put her in charge!"

Ripley, "You better just start dealing with it, Hudson.  Listen to me, Hudson, just deal with it because we need you and I'm sick of your bullshit."

After that talking by Ripley, you immediately saw him get his shit together.  It was a nice redeeming quality.
2. Frailty is one of the finest horror movies I’ve ever encountered. Absolute legend. 
3. https://preview.redd.it/uq1gue898p1h1.jpeg?width=1920&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=695160f2d849bb089ae2485293faf45bed86e8b4
4. Watched True Lies the other night. Career-defining Cameron collaboration 
5. The only man to be killed by a terminator, a predator and an alien!
6. Coconut Pete in club dread.
7. He was great in everything but I will always love…  
![gif](giphy|izZYf5BdV5WhowNlvW)
8. Twister 
9. Live Die Repeat.

## 5. Why don't they make real comedies like Step Brothers and Dumb and Dumber anymore?

**Score:** 400
**Body:** I love this movie genre. I grew up with Jim Carrey, Eddie Murphy, Robin Williams, Ben Stiller, Adam Sandler, Steve Martin, and all the other great comedy actors (I'm 40).

​The last real comedy movie I've seen must be from 2017 or something like that—so basically 10 years ago. How come they stopped making them? It's a really sad situation

 Do you have any recommendations for me from the 2019 to 2026 period? I mean really good comedies, like they used to make

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The death of DVD sales is a huge one. That is how a lot of smaller to middle budget movies turned a profit. They don't make the same money streaming. And that is where a lot of comedies sit. They are generally never big budget blockbusters. And the blockbuster is pretty much all the big studios want to chase.
2. The narrative I've heard is that the international market killed mid-budget comedy (as well as most rom-coms).

Big studios want to distribute everywhere to get the most bang for their buck. 

Humor tends to be culture specific. Action is more universal. Big studios are greedy and risk averse. For a while, they could sell The Avengers in China in a way they couldn't sell conedies.

That may be changing after markets like China got tired of Marvel movies in recent years. 
3. You mean its OK to laugh again at the movies?

Naked Gun last year was a real comedy
4. Just watch Always Sunny in Philadelphia 
5. I thought Ricky Stanicky (2024)was actually really funny. First comedy I've enjoyed in a long time.
6. The two big reasons are streaming and Marvel. Streaming killed smaller movies and nieched genres, while the Marvel movies took themselves seriously but also had lots of jokes, quips and humor sprouted in.
  - Streaming for sure. Even if comedies didn't do so well in theaters, they could make a ton of money on DVD sales. 
7. Because streaming and social media ruined everything good in this world.
  - There's still some good in this world Mr Frodo, and it's worth fighting for.
8. Nirvanna the band the show the movie (2026).

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Was shocked how good Kristen Stewart's The Chronology of Water (2025) was

**Score:** 66
**Body:** I let time pass after its release and actually forgot it was even made, even though I had been looking forward to it. A recent interview of her called it back to mind. I thought it was going to be a small, almost quaint, artistic film that had a personal aura about it. I was not prepared for the power of the emotion, the towering presence of a depressed, removed, pencil-cracking, angry main character (performance), and especially not ready for how absolutely inspired it was in terms of filmmaking itself. A Proustian, Sound-and-the-Fury, intensive exploration of memory and trauma. Usually I resist all sorts of flashback motifs and editing techniques, but this film completely invaded me with both emotion (as if it were my own memories) and sensation. Again and again I pulled into the familiar of what I have sensed in life, in the privacy of my own being. And the treatment of abuse and its after image...it dove so deep down into it. The closest thing I could think of was Aftersun (2022) which approached suicide and depression from an oblique angle of childhood and sensation. This began in that realm, but sent me so far into the maze of what makes a person, I was pretty taken aback that Kristen Stewart could make such a film. I think it **must** be watched on a big screen in a dark room, right up against all that sensation and affect. You have to be swallowed by it to really get what it is doing. You have to be drowning in it, jolted by the cuts, just as she was. Felt a little bit like getting into a theatrical car accident, in how it reverberates in me far beneath the cerebral, and even emotional layers. Very impressed upon.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. totally blew me away, I actually went back and saw it again a few weeks later, because as you say, seeing (and hearing) it in a theater is so immersive. really did not expect Stewart to burst out of the gate with such a singular vision. career-best performance from Imogen Poots too.
  - It's a freakin achievement. I don't really follow new "cinema" in any purposive way, other than subreddits and social media streams. I'm very surprised that this film didn't reverberate forward, just in terms of talk (in contrast, for instance, I - rightfully - have heard a lot about Aftersun, just in the air of things). It's really a cinematic achievement because so much was accomplished through the things that film can do in a unique way. Light, sound, character, cuts, time-jumps, and beneath that texture, atmosphere, voice, close-up, movement, all used with great dexterity, to create the effect needed. She was making me feel (and think - as my mind was always racing to catch up) things that I've never really felt before. I've felt them at times in films, it wasn't totally unique, but the way they piled on each other with a cumulative effect, the whole film building like a chaotic orchestration (which was the character's own life), was just cinema risen to the moment. And...she's only 36.

and yes, the performance way out there. To have the camera on you like that, and to have so much of the films constructed effects also to rely on everything in your eyes, voice and physical expression. Feels reminiscent a little bit of what Gena Rowlands did in A Woman Under The Influence, with just how present she was throughout, as a lifeforce. 
    - I think ultimately it was a combination of challenging subject matter presented in a challenging style + terrible distribution with zero marketing. even in NYC it barely had a run (2-3 weeks in 2 theaters); I was lucky to catch it a second time when an arthouse theater had a few showings the next month.

Stewart has said it was extremely difficult to find even the barest amount of financing and distribution. it's surprising given her star power – you'd think a distributor like Neon or Mubi would be salivating at sending her out on an enthusiastic press tour. compare that to her peer Jesse Eisenberg's directoral efforts, which are much more mainstream-friendly and thus he had an easier time getting them produced and released. 

good spot on the Rowland influence, I've heard Poots say that she's a primary influence. also amazing that she can sell a 25-year age range mostly on the strength of the performance! it could have had Oscar buzz if it was properly released and promoted.
2. Recently saw it and I was impressed. I think it fizzles out towards the end but the performance from Poots as well as the sound design is really something! I also thought of Aftersun, but in comparison that movie held a tight grip on me throughout while Chronology lost me somewhere in the middle towards the end. But I totally get the comparison. Havent read the book so I dont know how it compares, but definitely interested after the movie.
3. Oh that's funny I started reading this and was thinking "sounds a little like *Aftersun"*, which I literally only saw for the first time just like five days ago and absolutely wrecked me. 

Looking forward to this, not sure where I can even watch it though. 

I will say that I'm not surprised she made a good movie, if you listen to her talk about film she's on fucking top of it. 

## 2. Park Chan Wook reviews Frankenheimer's Seconds

**Score:** 39
**Body:** This year's Cannes film festival's jury president Park Chan Wook was once a film critic before his successful career as a director. Here I translated and going to introduce one of his reviews, John Frankenheimer's classic *Seconds* from his book *Park Chan Wook's Homage*.

*A cursed classic*. No words could better describe this film. The history of *Seconds* is literally a piece of drama. A best-seller novel bought by Kirk Douglas  and adapted by Lewis John Carlino - one of the best playwrights in his era. After the initial plan of Laurence Olivier playing a dual role was rejected due to him being lack of marketability, Rock Hudson and Jack Randolph - who was out of show business since having been blacklisted in the era of McCarthyism - decided to play each roles. And it was completed by the works of the most promising director John Frankenheimer and the legendary cinematographer James Wong Howe. It was the only American film selected as In Competition section in Cannes Film Festival but had to retreat with boos from audience and critics. Domestic box office results were devastating as well.

(Spoiler Alert)

>!It brings me tears to watch Tony visiting Arthur's house to see the lady who was once his wife. She recalls her deceased husband with no pity, unaware that the man she is talking to has been missing since the surgery. You can see how deadly he wants to get his life back while watching Tony who introduces himself as a friend of Arthur, saying how much he has loved him. Here it seems to me that Hudson is revealing his homosexual lover, and it is much more tragic considering he was gay and eventually died of AIDS. And it is much more realistic to see when Tony confesses himself as Arthur to his neighbors after getting drunk since the actor had to hide his homosexuality and constantly lie to public. Hudson thought it was an opportunity to show his another character rather than playing an usual romantic role and naively expected to win an Oscar afterwards.!<

It was 1966 in the era of neo-avant-garde and hippie culture and this film is filled with experimental boldness, still hard to imagine today. It has undoubtedly never been made and will never be in this dark atmosphere. And most of all, I would like to give an applaud to Hudson's painful once-in-a-lifetime performance and put *Seconds* as one of my all-time top 10 movies without any hesitation.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. Ray Harryhausen

**Score:** 37
**Body:** There are very, very few people in film history who could be accurately described as visual effects auteurs.

Ray Harryhausen is undeniable a member of that small club, and I thought I'd start the first thread about him in a decade.

We talk about Jason and the Argonauts as a Ray Harryhausen movie, not a Don Chaffey movie... we look at Harryhausen's films as a body of work, with his stop-motion creatures (not the directors or actors or screenwriters) as the salient feature.

To me, the fascinating thing about Harryhausen is that he had a massive impact on film history (influencing Godzilla, inspiring George Lucas, Peter Jackson, Tim Burton, pretty much every vfx blockbuster director of the past 50 years) as the special effects artist on low budget b-movies that generally aren't particularly compelling when his creatures aren't onscreen. 

There are some really good, entertaining movies in his filmography, but just as many where the creature battles are the only reason to watch. 

In other words, Harryhausen has a pretty unique place in film history. His name is synonymous with stop-motion animation; his creatures still have a charm, a personality, a presence even if they're technically 'outdated' compared to CGI. 

I had the joy of seeing him host a screening of The 7th Voyage of Sinbad a few years before his death; a signed copy of his memoir is still a prized possession of mine. 

I know Harryhausen isn't the kind of filmmaker who generally gets discussed on r/truefilm, but I think he did have a pretty major impact & brought a virtuoso creativity to his films. 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His impact was great however he got the technique from Willis H. O'Brien who was responsible for the stop motion animation in King Kong. Harryhausen trained under O’Brien and always cited King Kong and his main influence.
  - I was at a display of his work a few years back and it said that he actually created his own stop motion animations in his parents garage before he started working with Willis, who was a big hero to him. They even managed to dig one up, it was pretty well done.
2. There was a 16-year gap between King Kong and Mighty Joe Young, the later Ray worked on.  In between about all we got was the string of Halloween monster movies, mostly good stuff but hardly awe inspiring.  Then starting with Ray's work in the 50's we got the awe inspiring creatures done well, not the cheap looking creatures of Them! (still an excellent movie) or the film enhanced monster of Tarantula, which is average.

So without Ray's work on Mighty Joe Young and the movies that followed there is no telling what direction or how long it would have taken Hollywood to give us quality monster and sci-fi effects, so for that I am forever grateful.
3. Grew up watching Jason and the Argonauts and Clash of the Titans, and there was such a legible through line of Harryhausen’s work on each; incredibly influential to me as a burgeoning lover of film, beginning to recognize patterns in artists’ work and aesthetic.  
Another effects god who belongs in this conversation is Phil Tippet; I think he’s another one of the few people who could be called a visual effects auteur
  - i agree. i was fortunate enough to see mad god shown at a theater. incredible. jan svankmajer is another one that comes to mind.
  - I really don't like Clash though.  I find it unwatchable.  For several reasons but in going to call a lot of the miniature stuff... terrible.


https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=qyxphQSx9qk

The owl is cool.  But the rest is not great to terrible. 

I'm thinking of the opening scene of Star wars a new hope, whth the Star Destroyer.  That totally worked.   Difference in "non moving"/simple versus "organic" objects?  Difference in matting?   No water scale problems?   Easier lighting?  Better continuity of scale establishment? 

(Lsck of shitty lazy react shots?)

## 4. The Searchers question

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I just watched The Searchers and I have a question about the plot. After Martin and Ethan finally see Debbie in Skar’s hut, how come afterwards they don’t say a word about it until she comes running over the dunes. In this arguably perfect film, I was confused by how this didn’t click for me. Am I missing something?

On a side note: What are everyone’s thoughts on the film and the poetic last shot?  


EDIT: I'm glad everyone is sharing their thoughts about the film but I was particularly wondering if anyone had clarification on the plot point I mentioned above.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In the book, it’s very clear that they are concerned with getting out of the village alive. Scar has deliberately shown her to them, and of course they are hugely outnumbered. That’s why they pretend that they haven’t seen her to avoid the confrontation he’s trying to provoke.
  - I more mean after they get out of the village and are setting up camp: I found it odd they didn’t mention Debbie at all
2. I saw The Searchers when first released. I was a child. Watched twice more, and love story and the direction. Spending all those years searching for the kidnapped girl is a movie of its times (based on a novel) and it still is true today that the ties of blood family go very deep and even though Debbie was acclimated to the native tribe she grew up in, the family still wanted her back in spite of the difficulties. The last poingnant scene in the doorway expresses the lone life of the cowboy.
  - Actually Ethan has trouble getting in doors throughout the entire film. Doorway symbolism in the searchers is a whole thing.
3. It’s a thorny film. One interpretation I’ve read of the last scene is that the men who protect us can’t live among us. The harsh reality of the world requires ruthless, violent men  who understand what civilized society cannot. They willingly sacrifice themselves to exile in order to protect us. 

I can see that interpretation. It jives with Ford’s politics of the time. 

Ethan is racist and brutal. He thinks about killing Debbie because she’d been the wife of an Indian.He struggles with his racism but couldn’t escape his nature or his legacy. He had to leave. 


## 5. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (May 17, 2026)

**Score:** 3
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **The Killing (1956, dir. Stanley Kubrick)**

There's a real Coeny energy to The Killing. The central conceit of the film concerns the ability for small, ostensibly unpredictable events to unravel carefully made plans. Kubrick wants you to carefully understand just how meticulous this operation is and what the characters have and have not accounted for. It's a good framework for a movie, and it's, as to be expected from Kubrick, expertly shot. Even from this early point in his career, he's confidently throwing around long takes and striking compositions that feel too austere even for '50s noir. Unfortunately, while this film has something interesting to say in an interesting style, I don't think the plot or characters really match up. The dialogue and characters feel generic, and I struggled to feel invested in their struggles or personal quirks. This really drags down the pacing in the first half. The Killing is just under ninety minutes, but it feels longer than something like Barry Lyndon or Eyes Wide Shut. I suppose the nonlinear storytelling of the third act is revolutionary in its own right, but I've come to expect more than just innovations from Kubrick. Something can only be so technically impressive if the story isn't very engaging.

**'49-'17 (1917, dir. Ruth Ann Baldwin)**

This film has a bit of an identity crisis. '49-'17 is about a young man, Tom, who works as a secretary for an East Coast judge. In his youth, the judge had eked out a prosperous living in the west, and he wants to visit the town in which he used to live one last time before he dies. But the town is abandoned, so the judge orders Tom to bring in a new crop of residents to revitalize it. The first half is a pretty clever satire about the mythologizing of the American west and how arbitrary and mundane the idea of manifest idea is. Tom fills the town with down-on-their-luck performers who pretend to be old west settlers, something that feels reflective of the hot interest in westerns brought on by the advent of cinema. Larger points about the west aren't made, though. Around the halfway point, the judge comes to the town, and the film focuses less on ideas of community and history and more on paternity conspiracies and financial crime, common ground in 1910s American film. '49-'17 never turns bad, and I think it's still worth watching, but it unfortunately fails to live up to some interesting, transgressive ideas, which is disappointing.

**Resident Evil: Damnation (2012, dir. Makoto Kamiya)**

While Resident Evil: Damnation has the esteemed distinction of not feeling like a cutscene compilation, unlike its predecessor Degeneration, that doesn't mean that the story is good. Resident Evil has a robust political landscape, and it only passes inspection if you don't give it a lot of scrutiny. The last thing Resident Evil needs is a political thriller, and oh boy is this it. It's like Metal Gear with all the Kojima sapped away. Capcom's stellar character writing keeps Damnation a snooze fest until the ending, an indulgent battle between tyrants, tanks, and UAVs that made up a little for the hundred minute runtime, but not enough. Watching Damnation feels like waiting out nuclear winter in a Soviet bunker.

**Society (1989, dir. Brian Yuzna)**

A glorious pastiche of different '80s cinematic trends, namely slashers, teen comedies, and body horror in the style of David Cronenberg and John Carpenter. Society is the story of Billy, who just can't trust his high society family and begins investigating why he feels this way - and why his suspicions might not just be paranoia. Through a very careful, patient investigation, Yuzna explores many ideas about sexuality, emotional vulnerability, teenage angst, trauma and mental illness, family, class, and what an individual owes to their society. Yuzna is masterful at building tension, and the climax is nothing if not extremely satisfying. The film's final arguments are not as coherent as something like They Live, but that's the last thing you'll be thinking about. Creepy, erotic, visually striking, and funny, Society is one hell of a batshit crazy, entertaining movie. It's so confident about its ideas you'll wish that there was a whole slew of copycats.

**The Station Agent (2003, dir. Tom McCarthy)**

I love stories about broken people finding connection and meaning in each other, and The Station Agent delivers in spades. Peter Dinklage plays an unremarkable person who nevertheless catches many people's attention because of his dwarfism. When his only friend passes away, he moves to a train depot he inherited in the New Jersey hinterlands, where he meets and befriends many people. The drama of the film is very organic, emerging from the other characters and their relationships outside of their burgeoning friendship with the very prickly, anti-social Dinklage. Similarly, there's little sentimentalism. The town doesn't band together to help Dinklage; his friends help him, and more importantly he helps himself when he starts to realize the extent of his priggishness. Most of what you see in the film is someone trying to pry a few words out of Dinklage over some beer or Cuban food, and it's nice. The actors all do a lovely job, and McCarthy's laidback direction makes you feel like you're just relaxing with these people. And when whole scenes of people sitting by a food truck go by, that's exactly the kind of mood you want.

**Betsy Ross (1917, dir. Travers Vale, George Cowl)**

Betsy Ross was an eighteenth-century seamstress. She's commonly attributed with making the first flag of the United States of America, but this is completely apocryphal. Interestingly, this isn't something like Columbus discovering America or the lost cause myth, where it generated at the time it happened and had to be critically interrogated later. No one in 1776 thought that Ross made the flag, and there is absolutely no contemporary evidence suggesting that she did. The story originated in 1870, spawned by her grandson and somehow picked up by the nation at large. Vale and Cowl were in  a unique position to tell a story that couldn't be complicated by real life events. They could have made the most contrived, obviously constructed, cinematic story about this real person, and no one could really raise a protest because the core concept is effectively a myth already. But, miraculously, their Betsy Ross film is lethargic and convoluted. There's a lot of plot, and many events don't have immediate consequences for the characters. It feels strangely true to life, but not in the way The Station Agent does. Betsy Ross feels more like one of those Wikipedia biopics, like Ashton Kutcher's Steve Jobs movie where a lot of stuff is loyally adapted from the subject's life, but there isn't really a compelling plot to tie everything together. What does international espionage have to do with George Washington contracting Betsy Ross for a flag? I don't know, other than Ross's indomitable sense of patriotism. Keep in mind that this film was likely produced after American entry in WWI, so that would explain all the insistent jingoism. It doesn't help that it's so difficult to follow the story, and that the film isn't shot or lit especially well, even for 1917. At the worst of times, Betsy Ross is a headache, and at the best, it's a testament to lost potential.

**Ghost in the Shell (1995, dir. Mamoru Oshii)**

From an aesthetic, material, and philosophical perspective, Ghost in the Shell has a beautiful world. It's as detailed as something like Akira's New Tokyo or Blade Runner's Los Angeles, but it doesn't feel too derivative of either setting. There's a strong balance between the shanty town clogged with people and advertisements and the blank concrete towers, and the myriad canals and ruins subtly inform the viewer just how much has changed in the world. The technology is omnipresent, almost to the point of becoming overwhelming, but fans of the genre wouldn't feel too lost. As attractive and exciting as invisibility, integrated brain computers, and arthropodal tanks are, the philosophical cost is never too far out of view. The film raises heady questions about identity and humanity in a world that's moving recklessly beyond those concepts, something that rings true today. 2029 is just around the corner. The best scenes in the film are when Kusanagi and Batou talk about swimming and when Kusanagi entertains the Puppet Master's proposition about fusion. Scenes like these are the only times these characters really jump off the frame. The actual story of Ghost in the Shell feels a little lacking. It's a very efficient dive into the geopolitics of the world, and while it's interesting to see how technology affects all levels of society, the plot never resonates as much as when you watch Kusanagi sail down a canal and spot a normal woman who looks just like her, living a life the major can never know.
2. **Cairo Station** directed by Youssef Chahine (Egypt/1958) -  This is a melodrama clearly inspired by Italian Neo-realism.  Set entirely in and around the central train station in Cairo and the motley group of poor laborers and snack vendors who dodge the railroad authorities and argue and flirt with one another day in and day out.  The film’s protagonist is a sad, thin, limping newspaper hawker named Kinawi (played by the film’s director).  He is smitten with the loud and brassy drink vendor Hannuma, who tolerates him, but she is eager to marry a macho baggage handler named Abu Siri (who is meanwhile trying to form a union).   The heat, the noise, and the filthiness of the environment doesn’t bring out the best in any of these people (an exception is the pious muslim named Madbouli, who works a newspaper stand and hires poor Kinawi, even finding him an abandoned railroad shack to sleep in).

It’s a watchable film, notable for its sophisticated cinematography, but some contemporary viewers may find it all a bit frantic and exaggerated.  I’m told that Egyptian audiences at the time of its theatrical release were unimpressed and perhaps a bit put off by its grim tone and story.



**The Music Room** directed by Satyajit Ray (India/1958) —  This is a good film that might have been a great film.  Set in the 1920s, it is the story of a once wealthy landowner, his wife, and teenage son living in their crumbling ancestral estate on ever diminishing funds.  The protagonist is Biswambhar Roy, who has neglected his duties as a landowner, but has a genuine and passionate love for hosting musical evenings in his manor’s elegant music room.  It is not just the delight of the live performances that he finds so satisfying - he is also anxious to host his peers (all male) and retain familial pride through their appreciation and esteem.  When a family tragedy occurs, Biswambhar closes off the music room and refuses all invitations and social engagements for four years.  Eventually a visit from a disliked *nouveau riche* neighbor prompts him, out of spite, to reopen the room for one last performance by local musicians and a famed female dancer.  He invites his friends for the final time (he literally pays the musicians with the last money he has).  The performance is wonderful and the dancer is indeed mesmerizing.  But upon its conclusion Biswambhar has nothing left (except for two loyal servants and a fine horse he could never bring himself to part with).

This is a beautiful and moving film.  Unfortunately, it is paced so slowly that at times it feels amateurish (the first two of three musical performances are grating and *interminable*).  Also, the third performance with the dancer *is* terrific, but implausible, since the dancer is clearly *not* singing, despite our hearing a female singer throughout the performance (the small group of accompanying musicians are all male).  Lastly, symbolism is sometimes heavy-handed (example: wind from a strong storm blows through an open window and topples a small decorative ship on a shelf, which the protagonist sets upright - this same storm is the cause of the family crisis).  The revered actor Chhabi Biswas plays the tragic protagonist and he is truly great in this film (he’s in practically every scene).



**In Bruges** directed by Martin McDonagh (UK/2008) - This first film by an admired playwright is very funny and very violent.  The plot is simple: a novice hitman (Colin Farrell) botches his first job and the crime boss who hired him puts him under the watchful eye of a more experienced killer (Brendon Gleeson) and sends both to Belgium to lie low.  The older man is genuinely fond of his whiney and remorseful charge and delights in this impromptu vacation in the charming medieval town of Bruges. The younger man hates the place and passes the time drinking beer, chasing a local girl, becomes obsessed with an American dwarf actor in town for a movie shoot, and can’t wait for their boss to allow them to return to London.  Nothing goes well for any of these three, rather likable, criminals.  Great dialogue.  Two fine performances by two skilled Irish actors.  Great scenery.  See it.

---
